coredata.dc:title: Temperature sensing using sulfur-doped carbon nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Sadhanala, Hari Krishna
Senapati, Subrata
Nanda, Karuna Kar
coredata.dc:description: Temperature sensing at micro/nano scale with materials by direct fluorescence excitation is widely used in the recent years. Here, we report the green hydrothermal synthesis of sulfur-doped carbon nanoparticles (S-CNPs) from inexpensive precursors such as sucrose and sodium thiosulfate. The resultant S-CNPs exhibit luminescence both in aqueous and solid states. The variation of steady–state photoluminescence intensity of S-CNPs with temperature is investigated and the sensitivity of S-CNPs solid film with 355 nm excitation is found to be 2.63 × 10−2 K−1, which is slightly higher than that with 532 nm excitation (2.15 × 10−2 K−1). The activation energy is found to be 141.87 and 30.7 meV for 355 and 532 nm excitation, respectively. Similarly, the sensitivity of S-CNPs in solution is found to be 7.3 × 10−3 K−1 and the the range of temperature is wider than physiological range (288–333 K) which suggests that S-CNPs can be used in the biological cells temperature sensing as well. Overall, the results suggest that S-CNPs can be used as nano thermometers.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors also acknowledge IISc for the financial assistance in acquiring a 200 kV FETEM.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a and b) TEM and HRTEM images of S-CNPs. Inset of (a) shows diameter distribution of S-CNPs. (c) AFM image of S-CNPs and (d) height profile of S-CNPs arbitrarily selected in (c). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) XPS survey spectrum, and (b–d) high-resolution C 1s, S 2p, and O 1s XPS spectra of S-CNPs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) EDX analysis of as prepared S-CNPs. (b-d) TGA. 1H NMR and Raman spectra of S-CNPs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a and b) FT-IR and UV–visible spectra of S-CNPs. (c) Excitation-dependent PL of S-CNPs. Inset of (c) shows the optical photograph of the S-CNPs under 365 nm UV lamp illumination. (d) Time resolved PL spectrum of as prepared S-CNPs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a and c) Temperature-dependent PL of solid S-CNPs thin film at 355 and 532 nm excitation, (b and d) Intensity ratio as a function of temperature. Inset of b and d displays the sensitivity of S-CNPs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Room temperature photoluminescence of solid S-CNPs thin film with two different laser excitation. Inset shows corresponding optical luminescence images. (b and c) optical images of solid S-CNPs thin films at different temperature with 355 and 532 nm excitation, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) PL spectra S-CNPs solution at different temperatures (288–358 K) with 450 nm excitation, (b) Intensity ratio of S-CNPs as a function of temperature and (c) Plot of relative sensitivity of S-CNPs solution as a function of temperature at 450 nm excitation. (d) Stability of S-CNPs for temperature sensing in the range of 288–358 K. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a and b) Bright and dark field fluorescent images of yeast cells with S-CNPs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Temperature sensing using sulfur-doped carbon nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Research Centre, Indian Institute of Science, Bangalore, 560012, India

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Present address: Bar-Ilan Institute for Nanotechnology and Advanced Materials, Department of Chemistry, Bar-Ilan University, Ramat-Gan, 5290002, Israel.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Temperature sensing at micro/nano scale with materials by direct fluorescence excitation is widely used in the recent years. Here, we report the green hydrothermal synthesis of sulfur-doped carbon nanoparticles (S-CNPs) from inexpensive precursors such as sucrose and sodium thiosulfate. The resultant S-CNPs exhibit luminescence both in aqueous and solid states. The variation of steady–state photoluminescence intensity of S-CNPs with temperature is investigated and the sensitivity of S-CNPs solid film with 355 nm excitation is found to be 2.63 × 10−2 K−1, which is slightly higher than that with 532 nm excitation (2.15 × 10−2 K−1). The activation energy is found to be 141.87 and 30.7 meV for 355 and 532 nm excitation, respectively. Similarly, the sensitivity of S-CNPs in solution is found to be 7.3 × 10−3 K−1 and the the range of temperature is wider than physiological range (288–333 K) which suggests that S-CNPs can be used in the biological cells temperature sensing as well. Overall, the results suggest that S-CNPs can be used as nano thermometers.

We report the effective synthesis of green luminescent sulfur doped carbon nanoparticles (S-CNPs) from sucrose and sodium thiosulfate using the hydrothermal method. On account of high chemical stability and bio-compatibility, we explored the S-CNPs for temperature sensing and yeast cell imaging.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) have drawn tremendous attention in the last few years [1–3] due to their unique properties such as excellent water solubility, resistance to photobleaching, chemical stability, inertness, non-blinking, low toxicity, highly optical absorptivity and bio-compatibility [4–7]. Doping of CNPs with heteroatom can tune their optical and electrical properties [8–10], and significant attention has been paid to nitrogen-doped CNPs (N-CNPs) due to their catalytic activity [11–16], which is comparable to that of activated carbon composite materials [17–21] and high quantum yield [11,22] compared to other ones such as S-doped CNPs (S-CNPs). This is because of the difficulty associated with the incorporation of S into carbon nanostructures due to the large size difference. However, there are few reports on S-CNPs [8,23] and the optical properties are not well explored.

The accurate measurement of thermodynamic variable such as temperature is more important due to their wide range applications in electronics, medicinal devices and biology, etc. [1] Generally, thermometry relies on the changes in the properties of materials with temperature. Thermometry based on optical temperature sensors has gained significant attraction due to fast response, high selectivity, high sensitivity and low noise. Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy [24,25], scanning probe microscopy and Raman spectroscopy have been exploited in this regard and PL based nanosensors to measure the temperature in micro/nano scale is gaining popularity among the researchers [26]. Based on the variation of decay time or change in the steady-state luminescence with temperature, semiconductor quantum dots, dyes and photoluminescent polymers were used to monitor the temperature in the field of nanotechnology [27–29]. However, heavy metal quantum dots toxic in nature and not suitable to detect the temperature in living cells. Owing to less toxic, CNPs have been used to detect the temperature [27] recently. In this context, it may be noted that CNPs are alternative to inorganic semiconductor nanoparticles.

In this article, we report the synthesis of S-CNPs from sucrose as a carbon source and sodium thiosulfate as a sulfur source using facile and effective hydrothermal method. The synthesized S-CNPs emit green luminescence under UV excitation (365 nm) and quantum yield is found to be 3%, which is comparable to pristine CNPs synthesized from sucrose. These S-CNPs are used as nano-optical probes for the detection of temperature both in solid as well as aqueous states. The highest sensitivity of S-CNPs film with 355 nm excitation is found to be 2.63 × 10−2 K−1, which is slightly higher than with 532 nm excitation (2.15 × 10−2 K−1) in the temperature range of 83–373 K. The highest sensitivity of S-CNPs in solution is found to be 7.3 × 10−3 K−1 in the temperature range of 288–358 K. The temperature detection range is more than physiological range (288–333 K) which suggests that S-CNPs can be used in the biological cells temperature sensing.

Sucrose and sodium thiosulfate were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used without further purification. HCl and NaOH pellets are obtained from S d fine Chemicals Pvt. Ltd. All the chemicals are analytical grade reagents and used without further treatment. Milli-Q water is used in all the experiments.

In order to synthesize S-CNPs, 0.8 g of sucrose and 0.2 g of sodium thiosulfate are dissolved in 40 ml of Milli-Q water and stirred for 10 min to form the homogeneous mixture. Then, the whole colorless solution transferred to 50 ml Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave. The sealed autoclave was kept inside the muffle furnace and maintained at 180 °C with the ramping rate of 10 °C/min for 2 h. After reaction was over, the furnace was allowed to cool down to room temperature naturally. The obtained yellow color solution was centrifuged (4000 rpm for 30 mins) to remove the bigger particles and finally obtained supernatant used for further characterizations. For solid PL experiments, films are prepared by drop casting the S-CNPs on glass substrates.

The synthesized S-CNPs have been characterized by JEOL Transmission electron microscope (TEM) at 200 kV, PerkinElmer Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer, Hitachi U-3000 UV–visible spectrophotometer, Horiba Jobin Yvon fluoromax – 4 spectrofluorometer with Xe lamp as an excitation source, ‘X'pert Pro PAnalytical diffractometer for X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements, and Atomic force microscope (AFM) experiments using A. P. E. Research A – 100. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out to know the chemical composition of as prepared S-CNPs using Axis Ultra-165. Temperature-dependent PL of solid and liquid S-CNPs is measured using WITec micro Raman – PL system and Varian spectrofluorometer. Environmental Scanning Electron Microscope (E-SEM), Quanta FEG250, equipped with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDAX) is used for the elemental analysis of S-CNPs coated on silicon substrate. PerkinElmer thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and Time resolved PL (TRPL) are carried out for the study of stability and lifetime measurements of S-CNPs.

Quinine sulfate (QS) is taken as a standard and dissolved in 0.05 M H2SO4 solution and S-CNPs are diluted with water for maximum luminescence intensity to avoid the self-quenching. UV–Visible Hitachi 2900 absorption spectrometer and Horiba Jobin Yvon fluoromax – 4 spectrofluorometer are used to record the absorption and PL (355 nm excitation) of QS and S-CNPs at room temperature. The equation for calculation of QY of S-CNPs is given below
ΦN-CNPs = ΦQS (δN-CNPs/ δQS) (ƞQS/ ƞN-CNPs) (βQS/βN-CNPs),
where ΦN-CNPs and ΦQS are QY of S-CNPs and QS, δ, ƞ and β are an integrated area of PL spectrum, refractive index of solvent, absorption value.

Fig. 1 a shows the TEM image of spherical S-CNPs and the histogram of size distribution is shown in the inset. The size ranges from 2 to 4 nm with an average diameter of 3 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of S-CNPs shown in Fig. 1b reveals the high crystalline quality with sp2 carbon, and the lattice spacing of 0.24 nm is corresponding to (1120) lattice fringes of graphene [30]. The AFM image of S-CNPs is shown in Fig. 1c with an average height of 4 nm (Fig. 1d) suggesting that most of S-CNPs are of around 12 graphene layers.

Fig. 2 a shows the full XPS survey spectrum of S-CNPs with four peaks at 166.1, 285.3, and 531 eV related to S 2p, C 1s, and O 1s, and an additional peak observed around 228.2 eV corresponds to S 1s. It is noted that an atomic percentage of S (9.1%) in S-CNPs is more than the reported S-CNPs (4.25%) [23]. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 2b) is divided into four peaks at 284.6, 286.04, 287.4 and 288.7 eV reveals the presence of C=C, C=S, C=O, and O-C=O, respectively. The two peaks of S 2p spectrum (Fig. 2c) are at 164 and 168 eV, which is further deconvoluted into two peaks each. The peaks present at 163.7 and 165.2 eV related to –C-S-C bond and the peaks at 167.5 and 169 eV are due to –C-(SO2)-C- bond. The O 1s spectrum is composed of two peaks corresponding to C=O (531.8 eV) and C-O (534.5 eV) as shown in Fig. 2d. The atomic percentage (at %) of C, S and O is found to be 67.9%, 9.1%, and 23%, respectively, which is further supported by SEM EDX as shown in Fig. 3 a (C-67.34%, S-8.99 and O-23.67).

TGA is used to investigate the thermal stability of S-CNPs under inert atmosphere. Fig. 3b displays the TGA spectrum of S-CNPs and the weight loss of 3–5% in the temperature of 25–150 °C is observed that is probably due to evaporation of water molecules. Further steady weight loss is observed in between 200 and 540 °C, which could be attributed to loss of oxygen functional groups and completely decomposed at 800 °C. As shown in Fig. 3c, 1H NMR spectrum of S-CNPs is revealing the presence of different kind of environments and the peaks at 1–3 ppm, 3–6 ppm, 6–8 ppm and 8–10 ppm are due to sp3 C–H protons, the protons attached with hydroxyl, sp2 protons, and aldehyde protons, respectively. Furthermore, surface properties of S-CNPs are studied. The surface area, pore volume and porosity of solid S-CNPs are 5.0890 m2/g, 0.0022 m3/g and 0.012, respectively.

Raman spectrum of S-CNPs (Fig. 3d) contains major and characteristic D, G and 2D bands at 1361, 1551, and 2807 cm−1, respectively, that reveals the defects of sp3 carbon and graphitic sp2 carbon. Fig. S1 shows the XRD pattern of as-synthesized S-CNPs with a broad (002) peak centered at 22.3° suggesting that S-CNPs consist of disordered stacking with more surface active sites during the hydrothermal synthesis. The corresponding d-spacing of S-CNPs is found to be ∼0.38 nm, which is more than that of graphite (0.34 nm). Fig. 4 a shows the FT-IR spectrum of S-CNPs and it consists of peaks at 872, 1060, 1223, 1579, 1735, 2090, 2346, 2971 and 3642 cm−1 corresponding to = C-H bending, C=S, C-OR, -C-H bending, C=C, C=O, =C-H stretching, CO2, C-H stretching and O-H vibrational bands, respectively. The characteristic band at 1060 cm−1 suggests the presence of sulfur in the S-CNPs. These results unveil the crystalline and graphitic nature of S-CNPs and incorporation of sulfur atoms into the carbon-conjugated system of graphene.

Fig. 4b shows the typical UV–visible spectrum of S-CNPs in solution and it consists of peaks centered around 214 and 254 nm which are ascribed to the π-π* transition of aromatic C=C, and a shoulder at 334 nm corresponds to the n-π* transition of C-O functional groups present at edges of S-CNPs. S-CNPs showed the excitation-dependent PL behavior (Fig. 4c) due to inhomogeneous surface states/size [31–33]. Excitation-dependent PL materials are useful multicolor image applications [34,35]. S-CNPs exhibit green color under 365 nm UV lamp illumination (inset of Fig. 4c) probably due to the presence of more oxygenated functional groups (carbonyl and carboxylic) [36,37] which was further supported by XPS and FT-IR spectra. However, CNPs (prepared from only sucrose) showed cyan blue color under 365 nm excitation [38]. The red shift in the PL of S-CNPs is probably due to doping of heavy sulfur atom in undopded CNPs (Fig. S2). For the measurement of QY, QS is considered as a reference compound (ΦQS = 0.54) and QY of S-CNPs in aqueous solution is found to be 3% which is higher than the undoped CNPs (1%) [38]. Fig. 4d shows the time-resolved PL spectrum of as-synthesized S-CNPs at 500 nm excitation with bi-exponential fitting, which suggesting that PL decay proceeds through two relaxation pathways in S-CNPs [39]. The average lifetime of S-CNPs is found to be 0.73 ns. Further, we have investigated the ionic strength of as-synthesized S-CNPs with NaCl. As shown in Fig. S3, the PL intensity of S-CNPs remains unchanged as the increase of NaCl concentration from 1 to 200 mM, which suggests the stability of S-CNPs.

To analyze the effect of temperature on the PL of solid S-CNPs film, temperature-dependent PL analysis is carried out from the cryogenic range to above room temperature (83–373 K). Fig. 5 a and c show the temperature-dependent PL spectra of solid S-CNPs at 355 and 532 nm laser excitation in the range of 83–373 K. It is apparent that the intensity decreases gradually with an increase in temperature for both the cases. Fig. 5 b and d show the variation of integrated PL intensity (I/I0) with temperature, where I is the integrated intensity at various temperature and I0 is the integrated intensity at room temperature. The decrease in PL intensity with temperature may be due to enhanced thermally activated non-radiative channels of surface (trap) states [40,41]. At lower temperature, radiative recombination dominates over the non-radiative recombination due to strong electron – hole interactions which lead to the enhancement in the emission intensity while increasing the temperature electron-phonon interactions become prominent over electron-hole interactions that favors non-radiative recombination. As a result, the number of emitted photons decreases and hence, the emission intensity. Based on the variation of PL intensity with temperature, we have made an attempt to exploit CNPs as temperature sensor. The absolute and relative sensitivity (S and SR) of a sensor can be -evaluated by using following expression [42].
(1) S = d(I/I0)/dT and SR = S/(I/I0)

The sensitivity (S) of the sensor was evaluated from the plot of the variation of intensity ratio (I/I0) with temperature and plot of d(I/I0)/dT as a function of temperature unveils the sensitivity as shown in the inset of Fig. 4b and d. With decreasing temperature, sensitivity is increased and the maximum sensitivity is obtained at 83 K. The sensitivity of S-CNPs with 355 nm excitation is found to be 2.63 × 10−2 K−1, which is slightly higher than that with 532 nm excitation (2.15 × 10−2 K−1). Also, relative sensitivity can be evaluated and given in Figs. S4a and b where the maximum SR is found to be 1.41% K−1 at 393 K for 355 nm and 0.79% K−1 at 103 K for 532 nm, respectively. These results suggest that the absolute/relative sensitivity of S-CNPs are superior to the reported literature (Table S1) and well suited for low-temperature sensing.

The variation of PL integral intensity (I/I0) with temperature in the case of both 355 and 532 nm excitation in the Fig. 5 b and d shows that integral intensity decreases with temperature due to thermal quenching and is expressed as
(2)
I = I
0 /[1 + Aexp(E
a /k
B
T)]
where I0 is the value of I at T = 0 K, A is a constant, Ea is the activation energy in the thermal quenching process, and kB is the Boltzmann constant. By fitting the experimental temperature dependent integral PL intensity data according to Equation, the activation energy Ea is found to be 141.87 and 30.7 meV for 355 and 532 nm excitation, respectively. This indicates that the activation energy can be varied by simply changing the excitation wavelength and the sensitivity can be optimized [43].

Fig. 6 a shows the room temperature PL spectra and the optical images (insets) of S-CNPs at 355 and 532 nm excitation, respectively. The optical images taken at various temperatures are given in Fig. 6 b and c for 355 and 532 nm excitation respectively. It is clear that highest emission is apparent at 83 K and intensity decreases gradually with temperature. It is interesting to note that the intensity varies with temperature without any change of wavelength. Overall, the results indicated that the PL intensity decreases exponentially with the temperature in the range of 83–293 K. The existence of the strong electron – hole and weak electron – phonons interactions at lower temperatures suggest the presence of more mobile π electrons carriers in S-CNPs. Hence, the variation of solid state PL of S-CNPs with temperature shows similar to quantum dots (nanoclusters) [44]. Interestingly, it is found that S-CNPs displayed bluish-white emission in solid-state and green emission in the liquid state when excited with 355 nm (Fig. S5a). This is probably due to the increased ratio of intrinsic core states and surface defects (oxygen functional groups) as shown in Fig. S5b.

Fig. 7 a shows the temperature-dependent PL of S-CNPs in solution at different temperatures (288 K–358 K) with 450 nm excitation. As the temperature increases from 288 to 358 K, the intensity ratio (I/I0) decreases without any shift in the emission wavelength as shown in Fig. 7b. The absolute sensitivity is calculated from the plot of I/I0 as a function of temperature and the slope of the plot gives the sensitivity, which is found to be 7.3 × 10−3 K−1. Further, relative sensitivity can be calculated from the Fig. 7c and the maximum SR is found to be 1.3% (K−1) at 358 K. The decrease of PL intensity with temperature is believed to be due to increased non-radiative combinations by thermal activation [39,40]. It is found that temperature detection range is more than physiological range (288–333 K) which suggests that S-CNPs can be used in the biological cells temperature sensing. As shown in Fig. 7d, S-CNPs showed excellent stability in the temperature range of 288–358 K over 5 runs.

Based on strong luminescence, low toxicity and biocompatibility, S-CNPs can be used for the cell imaging. Cells such as breast cancer cells [45], EAC cells [46], HeLa cells [47], RBC cells [48] Escherichia coli cells [6,49] and yeast cells [50] have been labeled with CNPs. Here, S-CNPs are used for yeast cells labeling. The yeast extract peptone dextrose (YPD) medium is used for yeast cell growth. Then cells are incubated with 50 μl of S-CNPs for 24 h in 1 M phosphate buffered saline (pH – 7.5). Fig. 8 a and b show the bright and dark field fluorescent images of S-CNPs at 480 nm excitation, and it indicates that S-CNPs can be used in the biomedical and clinical analysis.

In summary, we have reported the facile hydrothermal synthesis of S-CNPs from inexpensive precursors such as sucrose as a carbon source and sodium thiosulfate as a sulfur source. The resultant S-CNPs solution exhibited excitation-dependent luminescence with a quantum yield of 3% and green color under 365 nm illumination. On account of high chemical and photostability, we explored the solid and liquid S-CNPs for temperature sensing based on their steady-state PL intensity change with temperature. The sensitivity of solid S-CNPs is excitation dependent. It is found that the sensitivity of S-CNPs solid thin film with 355 nm excitation (2.63 × 10−2 K−1) is slightly higher than that with 532 nm excitation (2.15 × 10−2 K−1) and the activation energy of S-CNPs sensor is determined to be 141.87 and 30.7 meV for 355 and 532 nm excitation, respectively. The PL of S-CNPs in solution also varies with temperature in the physiological range (288–333 K) and the sensitivity of S-CNPs in solution is found to be 7.3 × 10−3 K−1, which suggests that S-CNPs can be used in the biological cells temperature sensing. Overall, the results suggest that the sensitivity of S-CNPs is superior to the reported literature and well suited for low-temperature sensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors also acknowledge IISc for the financial assistance in acquiring a 200 kV FETEM.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

Supplementary Information available [XRD, FT-IR, and PL data of S-CNPs].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary Information available [XRD, FT-IR, and PL data of S-CNPs].
Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2018.03.039.
